summary:
Brazilian independence, declared on 7 September 1822, followed a path unique in the Americas: rather than a violent revolution against colonial rule, it came about through a declaration by the Portuguese crown prince himself, making Brazil a constitutional monarchy rather than a republic and ensuring a degree of institutional continuity unusual among newly independent American nations.

The foundations were laid when Napoleon's invasion of Portugal in 1807 forced the Braganza royal family to flee to Brazil under British naval protection. The arrival of the Portuguese court in Rio de Janeiro in 1808 transformed the colony overnight: Brazil's ports were opened to international trade (ending the colonial trade monopoly), institutions were established, and Rio became effectively the center of the Portuguese Empire. When the court finally returned to Portugal in 1821 under pressure from the liberal Cortes, Crown Prince Pedro remained behind as regent.

The Portuguese Cortes sought to reduce Brazil to its former colonial status, dissolving its governing institutions and ordering Pedro to return. On 9 January 1822 — celebrated as the Dia do Fico (Day of 'I am Staying') — Pedro declared he would remain in Brazil. After continued tensions with Lisbon, he issued the Grito do Ipiranga on 7 September 1822, proclaiming Brazil's independence and declaring himself Emperor Pedro I. The transition was accomplished with relatively little violence, though military campaigns were needed to expel Portuguese garrisons in the northeastern provinces.

Brazil's imperial period lasted until 1889, when Pedro II, who had ruled for almost half a century, was deposed in a military coup and a republic was proclaimed. The independence settlement preserved the institution of slavery — not abolished in Brazil until 1888 — and left the plantation-based social hierarchy intact, reflecting the conservative character of the independence movement compared to the more radical currents in parts of Spanish America.
